content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

BAGATELOS & FADEM
THE INTERNATIONAL BUILDING
601 CALIFORNIA STREET
SUITE 1801
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108

BARRY FADEM
PETER A. BAGATELOS

TELEPHONE
(415) 982-7100
FAX
(415) 982-1085

August 28, 1990

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Chief, Technical Assistance
Branch Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: "Regulatory Published Newsletters," Regulation Section
18225(b)(4)(C); Status as "Contribution"

Dear Jeanne:

This firm serves as legal counsel to a committee called "Yes
on 126 - supporting the Alcohol Abuse and Drug Education Act of
1990, sponsored by Beer, Wine and Distilled Spirits Companies."
This committee has various supporters, including associations
which publish regular newsletters, which are in turn distributed
to the members of each association. The members of the
associations in some cases also publish regular newsletters
which are distributed to the customers of the respective
members. The Committee wants to request the associations to
include in their regularly published newsletters an article
supporting Proposition 126 and recommending that the
associations' members include in their own respective
newsletters articles urging their customers to support
Proposition 126.

Last week, I contacted the Technical Assistance Division by
telephone to confirm that the expenditures made for this purpose
would be exempt from reportability under the exception to the
definition of "expenditure" contained in Regulation Section
18225(b)(4)(C). To my surprise, you subsequently informed Peter
Bagatelos of this office that the "regularly published
newsletter" exception created in the "expenditure" regulation
cited above did not apply, and that all expenditures for such
newsletter communications were reportable as non-monetary
contributions. Because neither Peter Bagatelos nor I understand
the legal analysis behind this advice, I am writing to request
clarification.

As you know, the Political Reform Act does not contain a
definition of the term "non-monetary contribution" or the term

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
August 28, 1990
Page Two

"in-kind contribution." However, the definition of
"contribution" contained in Government Code §82015 states that
"An expenditure made at the behest of a candidate, committee or
elected officer is a contribution to the candidate, committee or
elected officer unless full and adequate consideration is
received for making the expenditure." (emphasis added) The
cited language essentially describes what is normally referred
to as a "non-monetary" contribution, and necessarily requires an
examination of the term "expenditure." The term "expenditure"
is defined in Government Code §82025 in part as "a payment, a
forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or
an enforceable promise to make a payment, unless it is clear
from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for
political purposes . . ."

The term "expenditure" is further defined in Regulation
18225 (2 California Code of Regulations §18225). Subdivision
(b) of this section states that an expenditure "includes any
monetary or non-monetary payment made by any person . . . that is
used for communications which expressly advocate the nomination,
election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or
candidates, or the qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly
identified ballot measure."

However, subdivision (4) of this section states:

"Notwithstanding the provisions of this subsection, the term
expenditure does not include costs incurred for
communications which expressly advocate the nomination,
election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or
candidates or the qualification, passage or defeat of a
clearly identified measure or measures by. . . (C) A
regularly published newsletter or regularly published
periodical, other than those specified in paragraph
(b)(4)(A), whose circulation is limited to an organization's
members, employees, shareholders, other affiliated
individuals and those who request or purchase the
publication. This paragraph applies only to the costs
regularly incurred in publishing and distributing the
newsletter or periodical. If additional costs are incurred
because the newsletter or periodical is issued on other than
its regular schedule, expanded in circulation, or
substantially altered in style, size or format, the
additional costs are expenditures."

In the opinion of this firm, expenditures for a "regularly
published newsletter" which meets the description set forth in
Regulation §18225(b)(4)(C) are not "expenditures" as that term
is defined either in the Regulation or in Government Code
§82025. Because such payments do not constitute "expenditures"

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
August 28, 1990
Page Three

as that term is used in these sections, the payments also do not
constitute "contributions" as defined in Government Code
§82015. The plain language of the definition of "contribution"
does not encompass such payments because they are not
"expenditures."

Moreover, it has always been our understanding that the
other two items which are exempted by Regulation §18225(b)(4)
from the term "expenditure" are also commonly considered to be
exempt from the term "contribution." These are the exemptions
for "A regularly published newspaper, magazine or other
periodical of general circulation which routinely carries news,
articles and commentary of general interest" and "A federally
regulated broadcast outlet." (See, 2 California Code of
Regulations 18225(b)(4)(A) and (B)). If these two items do not
involve expenditures, when made at the behest of a candidate or
committee, and therefore are also not "contributions," we cannot
understand why the exemption would not also apply to "regularly
published newsletters." The same analysis should be applicable
to all three of those items, and we cannot understand why
newsletters would be singled out for separate treatment.

Because it appears clear to us that expenditures for
regularly published newsletters do not generally qualify either
as "expenditures" or "contributions," your telephone advice that
all payments for such newsletters are reportable confused us.
Therefore, we would appreciate written clarification of the
following questions:

(1) Are expenditures for regularly published newsletters,
as the term is defined in Regulation §18225(b)(4)(C)
reportable as "contributions" if made at the behest of
a candidate or ballot measure committee?

(2) If so, what are the legal analysis and authorities
supporting this interpretation? and

(3) If not, does the newsletter described in the first
paragraph of this letter qualify as "regularly
published newsletters"? For purposes of this question,
please assume that:

(a) the newsletter is published on its regular
schedule and is not altered in terms of scope,
circulation or otherwise; and

(b) the newsletter is distributed to customers of the
association members who have either requested that
they be placed on the newsletters mailing list, or
have been placed on the mailing list because they

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
August 28, 1990
Page Four

have become regular customers of the association
member.

We would appreciate your expedited response to the foregoing
questions, in light of the fact that the Proposition 126
campaign is now fully underway and campaign reports must be
filed no later than October 5 for the period ending September
30. In the event that you have any additional questions, or
require additional information, please do not hesitate to
contact either Peter Bagatelos or myself at this office.

Sincerely,

Wes Van Winkle
Of Counsel

WVW/mlq
